title: Amazon Web Services credentials should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don't need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: AWS Access Key ID detected with AKIA prefix. AWS access key IDs grant programmatic access to your entire AWS account including S3, EC2, IAM, Lambda, RDS, and all other services. Exposing these credentials allows attackers to exfiltrate data, launch resources, create backdoor IAM users, and cause catastrophic financial damage. Revoke this key immediately from the AWS IAM console and audit CloudTrail logs for unauthorized activity.

AWS Temporary Session Access Key ID detected (ASIA prefix). Temporary session credentials issued by AWS STS provide time-limited access to AWS resources with all permissions of the assumed IAM role. While these expire automatically, exposure during their validity window allows full unauthorized access. Revoke the session via AWS STS InvalidateTokens and audit associated CloudTrail logs immediately.

AWS Secret Access Key detected in variable assignment. The secret access key is the password component of AWS credentials and provides full programmatic access to all AWS services when combined with an access key ID. Exposing this key enables complete account compromise. Revoke immediately via the AWS IAM console and rotate all credentials.

AWS Access Key ID and Secret Access Key pair detected together. This combined credential provides complete programmatic access to your AWS account. Attackers can use these to exfiltrate data from S3, launch EC2 instances, create backdoor IAM users, access RDS databases, and cause unlimited financial damage. Revoke both keys from IAM console immediately and enable MFA.
